title: Condensation Trays
desc: The warm reservoir air feeds a line of condensers above the nursery channels, and those trays need cleaning before the runoff turns bitter.

steps.text:
Where reservoir warmth meets cooler stone, water appears again in quieter forms. Along the upper nursery run, broad condensation trays catch moisture from the humid air and drip it into sealed jars used for delicate washing, hatch work, and any task that benefits from water cleaner than what travels by open channel. The system is simple. The maintenance is not. Left alone, the tray lips crust over with mineral scale and the clean runoff turns slow and bitter.

The tray keeper walks you along the line with a bucket of jars and a hooked scraper. "We can clear the drip lips before the scale hardens," she says, "or collect the first clean runoff into fresh jars once the line opens again. One job makes the water clean. The other keeps it that way."

options.label: Scrape the tray lips and restore the clean drip
Collect and seal the first clean runoff in fresh jars

steps.text:
The scale comes off in pale curls, thin but stubborn, and each tray lip needs a careful hand so the formed edge does not chip along with the crust. You move down the line restoring each drip to a narrow even bead instead of the scattered leakage it had become. Behind you, the first cleared trays begin shedding water in steady threads that sound almost musical after the dry scrape of the work.

By the time the whole line is done, the keeper can run a finger beneath any tray and catch a clean cool drop exactly where it ought to fall. She does this twice with obvious satisfaction. "Good. Order at the lip means purity in the jar." In Mount Pillar, even a drip can be governed if enough creatures depend on it.

options.label: Rinse the scraper and leave the trays shining wet

steps.text:
Fresh jars matter because the first good runoff after a cleaning is the best of the cycle, clear and faintly warm, carrying none of the older mineral tang from a neglected tray line. You set each jar under the reopened drip points, shift them as the flow steadies, and cap them one by one the moment they reach the keeper's measure mark. It is quiet work, almost ceremonial, and the nursery run seems to approve by settling into a rhythm of clean dripping and careful glass.

When the final jar is sealed, the keeper holds it up to the light from the reservoir side and inspects the clarity. No clouding. No grit. No pale film at the base. She packs it into the rack with the rest and says, "There. Water good enough for beginnings." In a place responsible for so many beginnings, that is not a small standard.

options.label: Slide the filled rack into storage and close the line
